title: Acute Coronary Syndrome
subtitle: From Plaque to Treatment: Understanding the Complete Pathophysiological Cascade

sourcePolicy.statement: All content derived exclusively from open-access peer-reviewed publications and clinical guidelines

guidelines.citation: Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025.
O'Gara PT, et al. 2013 ACCF/AHA Guideline for the Management of ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction. Circulation. 2013;127:e362-e425.
Thygesen K, et al. Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction (2018). Circulation. 2018;138:e618-e651.

landmarkTrials.citation: ISIS-2 Collaborative Group. Randomised trial of intravenous streptokinase, oral aspirin, both, or neither among 17,187 cases of suspected acute myocardial infarction. Lancet. 1988;2:349-360.
landmarkTrials.keyFinding: Aspirin alone reduced vascular mortality by 23% (p<0.00001)

vignette.title: The Case
vignette.text:
A 58-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by EMS after being awakened from sleep at midnight with severe chest pain. He describes the pain as a crushing pressure radiating down his left arm and into his jaw, associated with profuse diaphoresis, nausea, and shortness of breath. He rates the pain as 10/10.

Past Medical History: Hypertension (10 years), Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (8 years), Hyperlipidemia (5 years)
Medications: Lisinopril 20mg daily, Metformin 1000mg BID, Atorvastatin 40mg nightly
Family History: Father died of MI at age 52. Brother had CABG at age 55.
Social History: 30 pack-year smoking history.
Allergies: Penicillin (rash)
vignette.sourceNote: Clinical presentation per Universal Definition of MI [universal-mi-2018]

physicalExam.general: Alert, anxious, diaphoretic, in obvious distress
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular. S4 gallop. No murmurs.

ecg.description: ST elevation 3-4mm in V1-V4, 2mm in I/aVL. Reciprocal ST depression in II, III, aVF.

questions.stem: The ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal changes in II, III, and aVF.
questions.question: Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?
questions.explanation: ST elevation in V1-V4 indicates anterior wall involvement, supplied by the LAD. The LAD courses in the anterior interventricular groove and supplies the anterior LV wall and anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why do V1-V4 indicate anterior wall involvement?
whyChain.answer: Precordial leads V1-V4 overlie the anterior chest, directly over the anterior heart surface. They detect electrical changes in underlying tissue.

whyChain.why: Why does the LAD supply the anterior wall?
whyChain.answer: The LAD arises from the left main and courses down the anterior interventricular groove. Diagonal branches supply the anterior wall; septal perforators supply the septum.

questions.stem: Understanding conduction system blood supply is critical for anticipating complications.
questions.question: The AV node receives blood supply from which artery in ~90% of patients?
questions.explanation: The AV nodal artery arises from the RCA in ~90% of people (right-dominant circulation) and from LCx in ~10%. This is why inferior MIs cause AV blocks more often than anterior MIs.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does this matter clinically in our patient?
whyChain.answer: Our patient has anterior (LAD) STEMI. The AV node is typically NOT in LAD territory, so high-grade AV block is less likely. However, bundle branch blocks can occur from septal involvement.

questions.stem: The patient reports pain radiating to his left arm and jaw.
questions.question: Which mechanism explains cardiac referred pain to the arm and jaw?

options.text: Direct innervation of the arm by cardiac sympathetic fibers
Convergence of cardiac visceral afferents and somatic afferents at spinal cord T1-T4
Vagus nerve carrying pain to both heart and arm

questions.explanation: Cardiac pain travels via visceral afferents to T1-T4 spinal segments, where they converge with somatic afferents from the arm (brachial plexus C5-T1) and jaw. The brain cannot distinguish the source.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: The heart develops embryologically from cervical tissue, retaining sensory connections to upper thoracic/lower cervical segments where cardiac sympathetic afferents enter the cord.

questions.stem: An S4 gallop is heard on cardiac auscultation.
questions.question: The S4 heart sound is produced by which event?

options.text: Atrial contraction forcing blood into a stiff, non-compliant left ventricle
Rapid passive filling of the ventricle in early diastole
Turbulent flow across a stenotic aortic valve
Closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves

questions.explanation: S4 occurs in late diastole when the atrium contracts against a stiff ventricle. In acute ischemia, impaired relaxation (diastolic dysfunction) makes the ventricle non-compliant.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does ischemia cause ventricular stiffness?
whyChain.answer: Relaxation is ATP-dependent (SERCA pump requires ATP for calcium reuptake). Ischemia depletes ATP, impairing calcium sequestration and causing persistent actin-myosin interaction.

questions.stem: The LAD supplies critical structures beyond the anterior wall.
questions.question: In proximal LAD occlusion, which structure is MOST at risk?
questions.explanation: The LAD gives off septal perforators supplying the anterior two-thirds of the septum. Proximal occlusion causes septal infarction, leading to conduction abnormalities and possibly septal rupture.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does septal involvement worsen prognosis?
whyChain.answer: The septum contains the bundle of His and branches. Infarction causes conduction blocks (RBBB, LBBB). The septum also contributes significantly to LV function.

questions.stem: The patient's heart rate is 102 bpm despite severe distress.
questions.question: What is the PRIMARY mechanism driving tachycardia in this patient?

options.text: Direct effect of ischemia on SA node automaticity
Baroreceptor reflex responding to decreased cardiac output

questions.explanation: Ischemia reduces contractility → decreased stroke volume → decreased CO → decreased BP → baroreceptor unloading → sympathetic activation → tachycardia and vasoconstriction.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Double-edged sword: maintains CO but increases O2 demand and decreases diastolic time (coronary perfusion time). This is why beta-blockers benefit ACS.

questions.stem: Blood pressure is 158/98 despite acute cardiac injury.
questions.question: What explains elevated blood pressure during acute MI?

options.text: Increased cardiac output from Starling mechanism
Acute kidney injury causing volume retention
Intense sympathetic vasoconstriction outweighing decreased cardiac output

questions.explanation: Massive sympathetic activation causes intense arterial vasoconstriction (α1 effect), increasing SVR. Since MAP ≈ CO × SVR, marked SVR increase can maintain BP despite reduced CO.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Elevated BP provides room for nitroglycerin (reduces preload, may drop BP). Hypotension (SBP <90) would contraindicate nitrates and suggest cardiogenic shock.

questions.stem: SpO2 is 94% on room air with RR 22/min.
questions.question: What is the MOST likely cause of hypoxemia?

options.text: Right-to-left shunt through patent foramen ovale
V/Q mismatch from pulmonary venous congestion and interstitial edema

questions.explanation: LV dysfunction increases filling pressures → pulmonary venous hypertension → interstitial edema → V/Q mismatch (edematous areas have reduced ventilation) → hypoxemia.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why no crackles if there's congestion?
whyChain.answer: Early congestion is interstitial (fluid around vessels/airways), causing V/Q mismatch without alveolar flooding. Crackles appear when fluid spills into alveoli.

questions.stem: Coronary perfusion timing is critical for understanding ischemia.
questions.question: During which phase does the LEFT ventricle receive most coronary blood flow?
questions.explanation: During systole, LV contraction compresses intramyocardial vessels, impeding flow. During diastole, relaxation relieves compression, and flow occurs. Tachycardia shortens diastole, reducing perfusion time.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is the subendocardium most vulnerable?
whyChain.answer: Subendocardium faces highest compressive forces (closest to LV cavity pressure), has longest vessels to traverse, and perfuses ONLY in diastole.

questions.stem: The Frank-Starling mechanism attempts compensation.
questions.question: How does the Frank-Starling mechanism compensate for reduced contractility?

options.text: Increased preload stretches functional myocytes, increasing their contraction force
Increased heart rate maintains output despite lower stroke volume
Blood flow redistribution away from non-essential organs

questions.explanation: When ischemic myocardium hypocontracts, blood accumulates → increased LVEDV → stretches remaining functional myocytes → enhanced contraction of healthy segments (Frank-Starling law).

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does stretch increase contractile force?
whyChain.answer: Moderate stretch optimizes actin-myosin overlap and increases troponin C calcium sensitivity. However, excessive stretch moves sarcomeres past optimal overlap, causing force to decrease.

questions.stem: Understanding the cause of STEMI is essential for treatment.
questions.question: What is the MOST common proximate cause of acute STEMI?

options.text: Rupture or erosion of a vulnerable atherosclerotic plaque with superimposed thrombus

questions.explanation: Most STEMIs result from rupture/erosion of a vulnerable plaque (thin cap, large lipid core, inflammatory cells), exposing thrombogenic contents, triggering platelet adhesion and coagulation cascade activation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Thin fibrous cap (<65 µm), large lipid/necrotic core (>40% volume), abundant inflammatory cells, and positive remodeling that masks severity on angiography.
whyChain.source: Fourth Universal Definition of MI. Circulation. 2018

questions.stem: Troponin I is markedly elevated at 0.89 ng/mL.
questions.question: Troponin release into blood indicates what cellular event?

options.text: Reversible ischemia causing temporary membrane permeability
Release from skeletal muscle due to distress
Irreversible myocyte injury with loss of membrane integrity
Active secretion by stressed but viable cardiomyocytes

questions.explanation: Troponin is bound to the cardiac sarcomere. Elevated serum troponin indicates myocyte necrosis with membrane rupture and release of intracellular contents—the biochemical definition of MI.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Cardiac troponin I and T have amino acid sequences different from skeletal muscle isoforms. Modern assays are specific for cardiac isoforms.
Ischemic cascade: O2 deprivation → ATP depletion → Na+/K+ ATPase failure → Na+ and H2O accumulate → cell swelling → membrane rupture. Calcium overload activates phospholipases damaging membranes.

questions.stem: At the cellular level, ischemia triggers a metabolic cascade.
questions.question: Within minutes of complete occlusion, what is the PRIMARY metabolic consequence?

options.text: Release of reactive oxygen species from mitochondria
Activation of apoptotic pathways via caspases
Shift from aerobic oxidative phosphorylation to anaerobic glycolysis with lactate accumulation
Immediate cessation of all ATP production

questions.explanation: When oxygen stops, oxidative phosphorylation halts within seconds. Cells shift to anaerobic glycolysis (2 ATP/glucose vs 36-38). Lactate accumulates causing acidosis, further impairing function.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is aerobic metabolism so much more efficient?
whyChain.answer: Oxidative phosphorylation generates ~36 ATP per glucose via ETC. Glycolysis produces only 2 ATP net. The heart uses ~6 kg ATP daily—cannot survive on glycolysis alone.

questions.stem: Reperfusion therapy is time-critical. The wavefront of necrosis concept is fundamental.
questions.question: Why does necrosis progress from subendocardium to epicardium?

options.text: Subendocardium has highest concentration of coronary arteries
Subendocardium has highest O2 demand, poorest collaterals, and greatest compressive forces
Necrosis spreads via electrical conduction pathways
Epicardial cells are more resistant due to higher glycogen

questions.explanation: The subendocardium dies first: farthest from epicardial supply, highest wall stress, highest O2 demand, least collaterals. Necrosis spreads transmurally over 3-6 hours—the window for reperfusion.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: At ~20 min, subendocardial cells die. By 3 hours, ~50% of at-risk tissue is necrotic. By 6 hours, necrosis is nearly transmural. Time is muscle.

questions.stem: The patient has multiple risk factors.
questions.question: Which process links diabetes to accelerated atherosclerosis?

options.text: Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) cause endothelial dysfunction and inflammation
Insulin directly stimulates smooth muscle proliferation

questions.explanation: Chronic hyperglycemia leads to non-enzymatic glycation forming AGEs. AGEs cause endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, inflammation, vascular stiffness, and promote foam cell formation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why is DM a coronary artery disease equivalent?
whyChain.answer: Diabetics have such accelerated atherosclerosis that their CV risk equals non-diabetics who already had an MI. Guidelines treat diabetes aggressively.

questions.stem: Reperfusion injury is a treatment paradox.
questions.question: Which mechanism is MOST responsible for reperfusion injury?

options.text: Mechanical damage from blood rushing into ischemic tissue
Reactive oxygen species from re-energized mitochondria and neutrophils

questions.explanation: Reperfusion causes ROS burst (oxygen returns to damaged mitochondria), calcium overload, inflammation (neutrophil infiltration), and mPTP opening. However, benefits far outweigh injury—always reperfuse.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why still reperfuse if it causes injury?
whyChain.answer: Reperfusion injury damages 10-30% of reperfused tissue. Without reperfusion, 100% dies. Net benefit is enormous.

questions.stem: Aspirin is administered immediately upon suspicion of ACS.
questions.question: What is aspirin's mechanism of benefit in ACS?

options.text: Dissolution of fibrin clot via plasminogen
Irreversible COX-1 inhibition blocking thromboxane A2 and platelet aggregation

questions.explanation: Aspirin irreversibly acetylates COX-1, preventing TXA2 synthesis. TXA2 is a potent platelet activator. Aspirin reduces platelet aggregation on ruptured plaque. Effect lasts platelet lifespan (7-10 days).

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Aspirin acetylates serine in COX-1 active site. Platelets lack nuclei—cannot synthesize new enzyme. Only way to restore TXA2 is new platelets (~10%/day).
ISIS-2 showed 23% mortality reduction. Aspirin acts in 15-30 min with minimal risk. Cost of missed ACS exceeds cost of unnecessary aspirin.

questions.stem: Nitroglycerin is given for chest pain relief.
questions.question: By what mechanism does nitroglycerin relieve angina?

options.text: Direct negative inotropic effect reducing O2 consumption
Blocking pain receptors in cardiac afferents
Nitric oxide release causing venodilation (decreased preload) and coronary vasodilation

questions.explanation: Nitroglycerin converts to NO → activates guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → smooth muscle relaxation. Primary effect: venodilation reduces preload → decreased wall stress → decreased O2 demand.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Wall stress = (Pressure × Radius)/(2 × Thickness). Decreasing ventricular volume (radius) decreases wall stress, a major determinant of O2 demand.
Avoid if SBP <90, recent PDE5 inhibitor use (severe hypotension), RV infarction (preload-dependent), or severe aortic stenosis.

questions.stem: Beta-blockers are cornerstone ACS therapy.
questions.question: How do beta-blockers reduce myocardial O2 demand?

options.text: Negative chronotropic and inotropic effects reduce HR and contractility
Direct coronary vasodilation increases O2 supply

questions.explanation: Beta-blockers (β1 blockade) reduce: HR (negative chronotropy), contractility (negative inotropy), and BP. Together, these dramatically reduce MVO2. Slower HR also increases diastolic coronary perfusion time.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Major determinants: HR, wall stress (related to BP/LV volume), contractility. Beta-blockers reduce all three. Rate-pressure product (HR × SBP) estimates MVO2.
HR <60, high-grade AV block, SBP <100, active HF with congestion, cocaine/amphetamine intoxication, severe reactive airway disease.

questions.stem: Anticoagulation is essential alongside antiplatelet therapy.
questions.question: What is the mechanism of unfractionated heparin?

options.text: Binds and potentiates antithrombin III, which inactivates thrombin and factor Xa

questions.explanation: UFH is an indirect thrombin inhibitor. It binds ATIII, causing conformational change that increases ATIII's ability to inactivate thrombin and Xa by ~1000-fold. Prevents thrombus propagation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why anticoagulation if already on antiplatelets?
whyChain.answer: Thrombus has two components: white clot (platelets—addressed by aspirin/P2Y12i) and red clot (fibrin—addressed by anticoagulation). Both must be targeted.

questions.stem: A P2Y12 inhibitor is needed. The patient is allergic to penicillin.
questions.question: How does ticagrelor differ from clopidogrel?

options.text: Ticagrelor works on venous thrombi; clopidogrel only arterial
Ticagrelor is direct and reversible; clopidogrel is an irreversible prodrug requiring hepatic activation
Ticagrelor is IV; clopidogrel is oral
Ticagrelor inhibits both P2Y12 and P2Y1; clopidogrel only P2Y12

questions.explanation: Clopidogrel requires CYP2C19 metabolism to active form, then irreversibly binds P2Y12. Ticagrelor directly and reversibly binds P2Y12—faster onset, not affected by CYP2C19 polymorphisms, but requires BID dosing.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: ~30% of Caucasians and ~50% of Asians are poor metabolizers, reducing clopidogrel activation. These patients have higher stent thrombosis rates on clopidogrel.

questions.stem: Long-term secondary prevention is critical.
questions.question: What is the PRIMARY mechanism by which statins reduce CV events?

options.text: HMG-CoA reductase inhibition → reduced hepatic cholesterol → upregulated LDL receptors → increased LDL clearance

questions.explanation: Statins inhibit HMG-CoA reductase (rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol synthesis). Reduced intracellular cholesterol upregulates hepatocyte LDL receptors → increased LDL clearance. Plus pleiotropic anti-inflammatory effects.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: High-intensity (atorvastatin 40-80mg, rosuvastatin 20-40mg) reduces LDL ≥50%. Secondary prevention goal: LDL <70 mg/dL. Lower LDL = greater risk reduction.

questions.stem: Before diagnostics, you must consider differential diagnosis.
questions.question: Which is the MOST important must-not-miss diagnosis to consider alongside STEMI?
questions.explanation: Aortic dissection is critical because: similar presentation (severe chest pain, diaphoresis), and treatment for MI (anticoagulation, thrombolytics) can be FATAL in dissection. Must consider before antithrombotic therapy.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Dissection: sudden tearing pain maximal at onset, radiates to back, BP differential between arms, pulse deficits, aortic regurgitation, widened mediastinum. Our patient's crushing pain to arm/jaw favors MI.

questions.stem: ECG shows STEMI criteria. Time-to-treatment is critical.
questions.question: What is the recommended door-to-balloon time for primary PCI?
questions.explanation: Guidelines recommend door-to-balloon ≤90 minutes for primary PCI. If PCI cannot be achieved within 120 minutes of first medical contact, consider fibrinolytics (door-to-needle ≤30 min). Every delay increases mortality.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Trials showed survival benefit maximized with reperfusion within 90-120 min. Beyond this, wavefront of necrosis progresses substantially. Mortality increases with each 15-min delay.

questions.stem: Patient will go to cath lab. Informed consent needed.
questions.question: Which complication is MOST serious potential risk of PCI?

options.text: Death, MI (new or extension), or stroke

questions.explanation: Most serious complications: death, MI (periprocedural or stent thrombosis), stroke (emboli/dissection). Rare (<2% combined) but must be disclosed. Risk of NOT intervening far exceeds procedural risk.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why might death occur during PCI?
whyChain.answer: Refractory arrhythmia, coronary dissection/perforation with tamponade, abrupt vessel closure, massive contrast reaction, stroke. Higher risk in cardiogenic shock or left main disease.

questions.stem: Risk stratification guides management intensity.
questions.question: Which feature indicates HIGHEST risk in this patient?

options.text: ST elevation (STEMI) indicating complete coronary occlusion

questions.explanation: ST elevation indicates complete occlusion with transmural ischemia—highest acuity ACS. STEMI has ~7-10% in-hospital mortality and requires immediate reperfusion. The ECG pattern carries highest immediate risk.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Killip class (I-IV) stratifies by HF severity: I = no HF, IV = cardiogenic shock. Higher class = higher mortality. Our patient is Killip I (clear lungs)—favorable despite STEMI.

questions.stem: Patient asks why he had an MI when he felt fine until tonight.
questions.question: Why can acute MI occur without warning symptoms?

options.text: MIs only occur in arteries with >90% stenosis that were always asymptomatic
Vulnerable plaques often have moderate stenosis and don't cause angina before rupturing
His diabetes prevented feeling warning symptoms
Adrenaline always masks symptoms until complete occlusion

questions.explanation: Vulnerable plaques typically cause 40-60% stenosis due to outward remodeling—no flow limitation, no angina. When they rupture, sudden complete occlusion causes MI as first symptom. ~50% of MIs occur without prior angina.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: His fatigue and indigestion over the past week may have been anginal equivalents—atypical presentations of unstable angina before complete occlusion. Often missed or attributed to benign causes.
whyChain.source: Fourth Universal Definition of MI. Circulation. 2018

questions.stem: The patient is at risk for arrhythmias post-STEMI.
questions.question: In the first 24-48 hours, which arrhythmia is the MOST common cause of sudden death?
questions.explanation: VF is most common cause of sudden death in acute STEMI. Ischemia creates electrical heterogeneity (different refractory periods/conduction velocities) setting up re-entry circuits. Risk highest in first 4 hours.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Ischemic myocytes depolarize, have prolonged action potentials, and conduct slowly. This heterogeneity between ischemic and normal tissue creates substrate for re-entry. Premature beats can initiate VF.

No. CAST trial showed prophylactic antiarrhythmics (lidocaine) increased mortality despite suppressing PVCs. Focus on reperfusion, beta-blockers, electrolyte correction, and defibrillator availability.

questions.stem: Mechanical complications can occur after transmural MI.
questions.question: When is ventricular free wall rupture MOST likely?

options.text: Day 3-7 when infarcted tissue is softest during macrophage infiltration

questions.explanation: Free wall rupture typically day 3-7 when necrotic tissue is degraded by macrophages/proteolytic enzymes—tissue is weakest. Before day 3: coagulative necrosis maintains structure. After week 2: scar provides strength.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Three major: (1) Free wall rupture → tamponade, (2) VSD → left-to-right shunt, (3) Papillary muscle rupture → acute severe MR. All present with sudden hemodynamic collapse days post-MI.

questions.stem: Long-term prognosis depends on several factors.
questions.question: What is the MOST important predictor of long-term mortality post-STEMI?
questions.explanation: LVEF is the strongest predictor of long-term mortality. LVEF <40% indicates systolic HF and predicts higher death, HF hospitalization, and arrhythmia risk. LVEF integrates the net effect of infarct size and remodeling.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Yes. Stunned myocardium (viable but dysfunctional) can recover over weeks-months after reperfusion. Early LVEF may underestimate eventual function. Re-image in 6-12 weeks.
Primary prevention ICD if LVEF ≤35% persists >40 days post-MI with NYHA II-III symptoms, or LVEF ≤30% regardless of symptoms.

questions.stem: Integration of basic science and clinical concepts.
questions.question: The patient received aspirin, ticagrelor, heparin, and primary PCI within 60 minutes. Which sequence was interrupted?

options.text: Demand ischemia → supply-demand mismatch → angina → MI.
Plaque rupture → platelet adhesion → thrombus → occlusion → ischemia → necrosis. Treatment prevented propagation and restored flow.
Atheroma embolization → microinfarcts → HF. Treatment dissolved emboli.
Vasospasm → ischemia → arrhythmia → arrest. Treatment reversed vasospasm.

questions.explanation: Type 1 MI cascade: plaque rupture → subendothelial exposure → platelet adhesion/activation/aggregation → coagulation cascade → occlusive thrombus → ischemia → necrosis. Multi-targeted therapy interrupted at multiple points.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: Hemostasis has redundancy. Platelets activate via multiple agonists. Thrombus has platelet (white) and fibrin (red) components. Multi-targeted therapy provides more complete interruption.

questions.stem: Comprehensive secondary prevention is needed.
questions.question: Which medication combination BEST addresses multiple pathophysiological mechanisms?

options.text: Warfarin, Low-intensity statin, Alpha-blocker, Nitrate patch
Aspirin only, Moderate statin, Calcium channel blocker, Loop diuretic
Aspirin + Ticagrelor (antiplatelet), High-intensity statin, Beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor

questions.explanation: Optimal post-STEMI regimen: DAPT prevents stent thrombosis (12 months), high-intensity statin lowers LDL/stabilizes plaques, beta-blocker reduces remodeling/arrhythmia, ACE-i blocks RAAS/prevents remodeling. All have Class I evidence.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.answer: After stenting, struts are thrombogenic until endothelialized (~12 months for drug-eluting stents). Early P2Y12i discontinuation risks stent thrombosis (40% mortality).

questions.stem: The wife asks about family risk.
questions.question: Which response BEST integrates genetic and environmental concepts?

options.text: Only males are at risk due to Y-chromosome inheritance.
Heart disease is entirely genetic; each child has 50% chance of MI at same age.
Familial clustering suggests inherited susceptibility, but children can substantially modify risk through lifestyle and preventive care.
Family history is coincidental; MIs are random events.

questions.explanation: Premature CAD has strong genetic components (familial hypercholesterolemia, Lp(a), inflammatory variants), but is polygenic and multifactorial. Genes create susceptibility; environment modifies expression. Lifestyle reduces risk ~50% even with high genetic risk.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Coronary Syndrome. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Rao SV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: How much can risk be modified?
whyChain.answer: INTERHEART study: 9 modifiable factors account for 90% of MI risk globally. Favorable lifestyle reduces CAD risk ~50% even in high genetic risk individuals.

keyTakeaways.point: ACS results from vulnerable plaque rupture triggering thrombosis—not gradual stenosis
Wavefront of necrosis progresses subendocardium to epicardium over 3-6 hours
Door-to-balloon ≤90 minutes for PCI; door-to-needle ≤30 minutes for fibrinolytics
Aspirin reduces vascular mortality by 23%—give immediately
Troponin release indicates irreversible myocyte injury with membrane disruption
LVEF is strongest predictor of long-term mortality
